preamble.summary: A psalm of David. The most sustained meditation on divine omniscience and omnipresence in the Hebrew Bible. The psalmist declares that God has searched him and known him completely — every movement, every thought, every word before it is spoken. He cannot flee from God's presence: not in heaven, not in the grave, not at the farthest reach of the sea, not in darkness. God wove him together in the womb, and all his days were written before any of them existed. The psalm then turns sharply to a plea against the wicked before closing with an invitation for God to search and test the psalmist's own heart.
preamble.remarkable: Psalm 139 is unique in the Psalter for its sustained philosophical depth combined with intimate personal address. It is simultaneously the most theological and the most personal psalm — cosmic claims about God's knowledge and presence are expressed as 'You know me... You are there... You knit me.' The theology is never abstract; it is always relational. The psalmist does not prove God's omniscience; he addresses the One who knows him. The womb passage (vv. 13-16) is one of the most extraordinary poetic achievements in ancient literature — it imagines God as a weaver, an embroiderer, a sculptor working in the hidden depths of the earth (a metaphor for the womb), crafting the psalmist's body with deliberate artistry. The closing invitation — 'Search me, God' — is remarkable because the psalm has spent twenty-two verses establishing that God already knows everything. The psalmist invites what is already happening, which transforms omniscience from a threat into a comfort.
preamble.friction: Verses 19-22 contain violent imprecation against the wicked that seems to break the psalm's contemplative tone. Some scholars view these verses as a later addition; others see them as integral — the psalmist, having meditated on God's total knowledge, logically asks why those who oppose this God are allowed to persist. The shift is jarring but theologically coherent: if God truly knows everything, then the wicked are not merely committing crimes but rebelling against the all-knowing One. Verse 16 is textually difficult — the Hebrew of golem ('unformed substance') and the phrase about days written in a book is compressed and partially obscure, leading to significant translation divergence.
preamble.connections: The omniscience theme connects to Psalm 44:21 ('He knows the secrets of the heart') and Jeremiah 17:10 ('I the LORD search the heart'). The womb imagery parallels Job 10:8-12 (God forming Job's body) and Jeremiah 1:5 ('Before I formed you in the womb I knew you'). The 'wings of the dawn' (v. 9) echoes Malachi 4:2 ('the sun of righteousness with healing in its wings'). The closing plea to be searched connects back to the opening declaration of being known, forming a circular structure. The psalm anticipates Romans 8:38-39 (nothing can separate us from God's love) and Acts 17:27-28 ('in Him we live and move and have our being').

verses.text_hebrew:
לַמְנַצֵּ֗חַ לְדָוִ֥ד מִזְמ֫וֹר
יְהוָ֥ה חֲקַרְתַּ֖נִי וַתֵּדָֽע׃
verses.text_kjv: O LORD, thou hast searched me, and known me.
verses.rendering:
For the music director. Of David. A psalm.
LORD, You have searched me
and You know me.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm opens with two verbs that establish its entire theme: chaqartani ('You have searched me') and vatteda ('and You know'). The verb chaqar means 'to search, investigate, explore, dig into' — it is used for mining precious metals from the earth (Job 28:3) and for the thorough examination of a matter (Proverbs 25:2). God's knowledge of the psalmist is not casual acquaintance but exhaustive investigation. The second verb yada ('to know') in its completed form (vatteda) means the search has reached its conclusion: God has investigated and now knows — completely, finally, without remainder. The direct object is simply 'me' — not my deeds, not my words, but me. The entire person has been searched and is known.
translator_notes: The verb chaqar ('to search, probe, investigate') appears in Job 28:3 for mining ore from the earth — digging deep to find what is hidden. Applied to God's knowledge of a person, it implies God has gone to the deepest levels of the psalmist's being. This is not surface knowledge but excavation.

key_terms.semantic_range: to search out, investigate, probe, explore, examine thoroughly; to dig into, to mine
key_terms.note: The root implies thoroughness and depth — this is not a glance but an excavation. When God chaqars a person, He uncovers what is hidden beneath all layers of performance, self-deception, and concealment.

verses.text_hebrew:
אַתָּ֣ה יָ֭דַעְתָּ שִׁבְתִּ֣י וְקוּמִ֑י
בַּ֥נְתָּה לְ֝רֵעִ֗י מֵרָחֽוֹק׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou knowest my downsitting and mine uprising, thou understandest my thought afar off.
verses.rendering:
You know when I sit and when I rise;
You understand my thoughts from far away.
verses.expanded_rendering: God's knowledge is specified in two dimensions: the physical (shivti ve-qumi, 'my sitting and my rising' — the most ordinary human movements, the daily rhythm of rest and activity) and the mental (re'i, 'my thought, my purpose, my intent'). The word re'i comes from ra'ah ('to shepherd, to tend, to care for') or from re'a ('thought, purpose') — either reading yields depth: God tends the psalmist's thoughts as a shepherd tends sheep, or God perceives the psalmist's inner purposes. The phrase me-rachoq ('from far away') is not distance but advance knowledge — God understands the thought before it fully forms, from a distance in time or space.
translator_notes: The merism 'sitting and rising' covers all of daily life — everything between lying down and standing up, which is to say everything. It is the Hebrew way of saying 'every moment of your existence.' The word re'i is textually linked to either 'thought/purpose' or 'shepherding' — both meanings are theologically rich and may be deliberately ambiguous.

verses.text_hebrew:
אׇרְחִ֣י וְרִבְעִ֣י זֵרִ֑יתָ
וְכׇל־דְּרָכַ֥י הִסְכַּֽנְתָּה׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou compassest my path and my lying down, and art acquainted with all my ways.
verses.rendering:
You sift my traveling and my resting;
You are familiar with all my ways.
translator_notes: The verb zerita ('You have sifted, winnowed, scattered') is an agricultural term — winnowing grain to separate wheat from chaff. God sifts the psalmist's life, examining each component. The words orchi ('my path, my walking') and riv'i ('my lying down, my resting') form another merism: movement and stillness, waking and sleeping. The verb hiskantah ('You are familiar with, You are accustomed to') means intimate, habitual knowledge — not newly discovered but long known.

verses.text_hebrew:
כִּ֤י אֵ֣ין מִ֭לָּה בִּלְשׁוֹנִ֑י
הֵ֥ן יְ֝הוָ֗ה יָדַ֥עְתָּ כֻלָּֽהּ׃
verses.text_kjv: For there is not a word in my tongue, but, lo, O LORD, thou knowest it altogether.
verses.rendering:
Before a word is on my tongue,
LORD, You know it completely.
verses.expanded_rendering: The claim reaches its sharpest point: ein millah bi-leshoni ('there is not a word on my tongue') — the word has not yet been spoken, has perhaps not yet been fully formed — hen YHWH yadata khullah ('behold, LORD, You know it entirely'). God's knowledge outpaces human speech. He knows the word before the tongue shapes it, the sentence before the mouth opens. This is not merely foreknowledge but intimate familiarity with the psalmist's inner process of thought becoming language.
translator_notes: The particle hen ('behold, look!') expresses astonishment — the psalmist is amazed by what he is saying. The word khullah ('all of it, entirely, completely') emphasizes that God's pre-knowledge is not partial or approximate but total.

verses.text_hebrew:
אָח֣וֹר וָקֶ֣דֶם צַרְתָּ֑נִי
וַתָּ֖שֶׁת עָלַ֣י כַּפֶּֽכָה׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou hast beset me behind and before, and laid thine hand upon me.
verses.rendering:
Behind and before, You hem me in;
You lay Your hand upon me.
verses.expanded_rendering: The verb tsartani ('You have hemmed me in, enclosed me, besieged me') comes from tsur ('to bind, to press, to lay siege'). God surrounds the psalmist — achor va-qedem ('behind and in front'), cutting off every direction. Then the physical touch: vattashet alai kappekha ('You have placed Your palm/hand upon me'). The verb shat means 'to place, to set' and the kaf is the open palm — God's hand rests on the psalmist like a father's hand on a child's head. The image holds together constraint and comfort: the psalmist is hemmed in (which could feel like imprisonment) and touched (which could feel like blessing). The ambiguity is the point — total divine knowledge is simultaneously overwhelming and intimate.
translator_notes: The word tsartani is from the same root as tsarah ('trouble, distress') — being hemmed in by God can feel like distress. But the laying of the hand (kaf) is a gesture of blessing, protection, and ownership. The verse holds both the threat and the comfort of being completely known.

verses.text_hebrew:
פלאיה (פְּלִ֣יאָ֣ה) דַ֣עַת מִמֶּ֑נִּי
נִ֝שְׂגְּבָ֗ה לֹא־א֥וּכַל לָֽהּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Such knowledge is too wonderful for me; it is high, I cannot attain unto it.
verses.rendering:
Such knowledge is too wondrous for me —
too high; I cannot reach it.
translator_notes: The adjective peliah ('wondrous, extraordinary, beyond comprehension') is from the same root as niflaot ('wonders') in Psalm 131:1. There, the psalmist renounced walking in things too wondrous for him; here, he encounters the most wondrous thing of all — God's knowledge of him — and confesses he cannot grasp it. The verb nisgavah ('it is set high, elevated, inaccessible') and lo ukhal lah ('I cannot attain/reach it') together convey a knowledge that exists but transcends human comprehension.

verses.text_hebrew:
אָ֭נָ֥ה אֵלֵ֣ךְ מֵרוּחֶ֑ךָ
וְ֝אָ֗נָה מִפָּנֶ֥יךָ אֶבְרָֽח׃
verses.text_kjv: Whither shall I go from thy spirit? or whither shall I flee from thy presence?
verses.rendering:
Where can I go from Your Spirit?
Where can I flee from Your presence?
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm pivots from omniscience to omnipresence. The question anah elekh me-ruchekha ('where can I go from Your Spirit?') is rhetorical — there is no such place. The parallel anah mippanekha evrach ('where can I flee from Your face/presence?') intensifies with the verb barach ('to flee, to escape'). The psalmist is not trying to escape; he is testing the limits of God's presence and finding there are none. The word ruach ('Spirit') is God's active, pursuing presence — not a distant surveillance but a near, breathing reality. The word panim ('face, presence') is the most intimate term for divine encounter. The following verses (8-12) will attempt the experiment: what if I go up? down? east? into darkness?
translator_notes: The question format does not express desire to flee but astonishment at the impossibility of doing so. Jonah 1:3 narrates an actual attempt to flee from God's presence — this psalm explains why such flight is futile.

verses.text_hebrew:
אִם־אֶסַּ֣ק שָׁ֭מַיִם שָׁ֣ם אָ֑תָּה
וְאַצִּ֖יעָה שְּׁא֣וֹל הִנֶּֽךָּ׃
verses.text_kjv: If I ascend up into heaven, thou art there: if I make my bed in hell, behold, thou art there.
verses.rendering:
If I ascend to heaven, You are there.
If I make my bed in Sheol — there You are.
verses.expanded_rendering: The first test of omnipresence spans the vertical axis of ancient cosmology: shamayim ('heaven,' the highest point) to Sheol ('the underworld, the realm of the dead,' the lowest point). The verb essaq ('I ascend') reaches upward; atsi'ah ('I make my bed, I spread out a couch') descends into the grave. In both extremes — the ceiling and the floor of the universe — sham attah ('You are there') and hinnekka ('there You are'). The claim about Sheol is theologically significant: much of the Hebrew Bible treats Sheol as a place where God's praise ceases (Psalm 6:5, 88:5). But here, God's presence reaches even into the realm of death. The dead may not praise God, but God is present with them.
translator_notes: Sheol is not hell in the later Christian sense but the shadowy realm of the dead — a place of silence, stillness, and diminished existence (cf. Ecclesiastes 9:10). The claim that God is present even there pushes against other psalmic texts that describe Sheol as a place cut off from God (Psalm 88:5-6). Psalm 139 extends divine omnipresence to its absolute limit.

verses.text_hebrew:
אֶשָּׂ֣א כַנְפֵי־שָׁ֑חַר
אֶ֝שְׁכְּנָ֗ה בְּאַחֲרִ֥ית יָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: If I take the wings of the morning, and dwell in the uttermost parts of the sea;
verses.rendering:
If I rise on the wings of the dawn,
if I settle at the far end of the sea —
verses.expanded_rendering: The second test spans the horizontal axis: from the extreme east (kanfei shachar, 'the wings of the dawn' — the place where the first light appears, imagined as having wings that spread across the sky) to the extreme west (acharit yam, 'the farthest reach of the sea' — the Mediterranean, the western boundary of the known world, where the sun sets). The imagery is breathtaking: the psalmist imagines riding the dawn itself as it races westward, or settling beyond the farthest shore of the western sea. Even there...
translator_notes: The 'wings of the dawn' (kanfei shachar) is one of the most beautiful images in the Psalter. The dawn was imagined as a bird spreading its wings across the sky, its light extending from horizon to horizon. To take its wings is to move at the speed of light itself. Acharit yam ('the end/farthest part of the sea') is the western edge of the world in Israelite geography — beyond the Mediterranean, beyond everything known.

verses.text_hebrew:
גַּם־שָׁ֭ם יָדְךָ֣ תַנְחֵ֑נִי
וְ֝תֹאחֲזֵ֗נִי יְמִינֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Even there shall thy hand lead me, and thy right hand shall hold me.
verses.rendering:
even there Your hand would guide me,
and Your right hand would hold me.
translator_notes: The verbs tancheni ('would guide me,' from nachah, 'to lead, guide') and tochazeni ('would hold me, seize me,' from achaz, 'to grasp, take hold of') transform omnipresence from a philosophical concept into personal care. God is not merely present at the farthest reaches — He is active there, guiding and holding. The right hand (yamin) is the hand of skill, strength, and favor.

verses.text_hebrew:
וָ֭אֹמַר אַךְ־חֹ֣שֶׁךְ יְשׁוּפֵ֑נִי
וְ֝לַ֗יְלָה א֣וֹר בַּעֲדֵֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: If I say, Surely the darkness shall cover me; even the night shall be light about me.
verses.rendering:
If I say, "Surely darkness will overwhelm me,
and the light around me will become night" —
translator_notes: The verb yeshufeni ('will overwhelm me, crush me, bruise me') is the same rare verb from Genesis 3:15, where the serpent strikes the heel. Darkness is imagined as an attacking force, not merely an absence of light. The psalmist's desperate hope — that darkness could hide him — is about to be overturned.

verses.text_hebrew:
גַּם־חֹ֭שֶׁךְ לֹא־יַחְשִׁ֣יךְ מִמֶּ֑ךָ
וְ֝לַ֗יְלָה כַּיּ֥וֹם יָאִ֥יר
כַּחֲשֵׁיכָ֥ה כָּאוֹרָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Yea, the darkness hideth not from thee; but the night shineth as the day: the darkness and the light are both alike to thee.
verses.expanded_rendering: The answer to the darkness test: gam choshekh lo yachshikh mimmekha ('even darkness does not make darkness before You'). The wordplay is deliberate — darkness cannot darken God's vision. The night (lailah) is as bright as day (ka-yom ya'ir) to God's sight. The final statement is absolute: ka-chasheikhah ka-orah ('as the darkness, so the light') — they are identical to God. The distinction between light and dark, which governs all human experience (and was the first act of creation in Genesis 1:3-4), does not apply to God's perception. He sees in the dark as He sees in the light. There is no hiding place.
translator_notes: The theological implications are enormous: if darkness and light are identical to God, then the categories humans use to hide (night, secrecy, concealment) have no force before Him. This dissolves the fundamental human experience of being unseen in the dark. It also means that God's creative and sustaining work continues uninterrupted by night — He does not sleep (Psalm 121:4) and He does not squint.

verses.text_hebrew:
כִּי־אַ֭תָּה קָנִ֣יתָ כִלְיֹתָ֑י
תְּ֝סֻכֵּ֗נִי בְּבֶ֣טֶן אִמִּֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: For thou hast possessed my reins: thou hast covered me in my mother's womb.
verses.rendering:
For You created my inmost parts;
You wove me together in my mother's womb.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm now explains why God's knowledge is so total: He made the psalmist. The verb qanita ('You created, acquired, possessed') can mean 'to create' (as in Genesis 14:19, qoneh shamayim va-arets, 'creator of heaven and earth') or 'to acquire, to possess.' Both senses apply: God created the psalmist's innermost being and therefore possesses it. The kilyotai ('my kidneys, my reins') are the Hebrew locus of deepest emotion and moral sensitivity — the part of a person that feels most intensely. God created that organ of feeling. The verb tesukkeni ('You wove me, covered me, knit me together') uses the image of weaving — God as the weaver who intertwined the psalmist's body in the womb (beten immi, 'the belly of my mother'). The knowledge of verses 1-12 is not surveillance but intimacy: the maker knows the made.
translator_notes: The word kilyot ('kidneys') functions in Hebrew the way 'heart' functions in English — as the seat of deep emotion, conscience, and moral sensitivity. Jeremiah 17:10: 'I the LORD search the heart and test the kidneys.' God created the organ He now searches.

The verb sakakh in the piel (tesukkeni) means 'to weave together, to knit, to intertwine.' Some connect it to sukkah ('booth, shelter'), yielding 'You sheltered me.' We follow the weaving interpretation because it connects to the embroidery image in verse 15.

key_terms.semantic_range: kidneys; the seat of deepest emotion, conscience, moral sensitivity, inner life
key_terms.note: The kidneys were understood as the most hidden, most sensitive organs — buried deep in the body, impossible to see. They represent the part of a person that only God can access. When God is said to 'test the kidneys' (Jeremiah 11:20), it means He examines what is most concealed.

verses.text_hebrew:
אוֹדְךָ֗ עַ֤ל כִּ֥י נוֹרָא֗וֹת נִ֫פְלֵ֥יתִי
נִפְלָאִ֥ים מַעֲשֶׂ֑יךָ
וְ֝נַפְשִׁ֗י יֹדַ֥עַת מְאֹֽד׃
verses.text_kjv: I will praise thee; for I am fearfully and wonderfully made: marvellous are thy works; and that my soul knoweth right well.
verses.rendering:
I thank You, for I am awesomely, wondrously made.
Your works are wondrous,
and my soul knows it well.
verses.expanded_rendering: The response to being known is worship: odekha ('I thank You'). The reason: ki noraot nifleiti ('because I am fearfully/awesomely, wondrously made'). The word noraot ('fearfully, awesomely') is from yare ('to fear') — the psalmist's own existence inspires awe. Nifleiti ('I am wonderfully made, I am set apart as a wonder') is the niphal of pala — the same root as the wonders of God in the Exodus. The psalmist is himself a wonder, a divine marvel. Nifla'im ma'asekha ('Your works are wondrous') extends the claim from the individual to all of God's creation. The closing phrase ve-nafshi yoda'at me'od ('and my soul knows it very well') grounds the theology in experience — this is not merely doctrine but personal knowledge. The soul that God knows (v. 1) now knows something about God.
translator_notes: The phrase noraot nifleiti is more intense than the familiar 'fearfully and wonderfully made.' Noraot carries the sense of awe and even terror — the psalmist's existence is awe-inspiring, not merely 'nice.' Nifleiti means he is himself a wonder-work, a marvel on the level of God's mighty acts. Every human being, the psalm implies, is a divine wonder.

verses.text_hebrew:
לֹא־נִכְחַ֥ד עׇצְמִ֗י מִ֫מֶּ֥ךָּ
אֲשֶׁר־עֻשֵּׂ֥יתִי בַסֵּ֑תֶר
רֻ֝קַּ֗מְתִּי בְּתַחְתִּיּ֥וֹת אָֽרֶץ׃
verses.text_kjv: My substance was not hid from thee, when I was made in secret, and curiously wrought in the lowest parts of the earth.
verses.rendering:
My frame was not hidden from You
when I was made in secret,
embroidered in the depths of the earth.
verses.expanded_rendering: The verb nikhchad ('was hidden') is denied: nothing about the psalmist's formation was concealed from God, even though the womb is by definition a hidden place. The phrase asher usseiti ba-seter ('when I was made in secret') acknowledges that human formation happens out of sight — no one sees the embryo being built. But God sees. The most extraordinary image follows: ruqqamti be-tachtiyyot arets ('I was embroidered in the depths of the earth'). The verb ruqqam means 'to embroider, to weave in colors, to variegate' — the language of textile artistry applied to the human body. The 'depths of the earth' (tachtiyyot arets) is a metaphor for the womb, drawing on the ancient association between the womb and the earth's interior (cf. Job 1:21, 'naked I came from my mother's womb'). The psalmist's body was not merely assembled but embroidered — crafted with the care and artistry of a master textile worker producing a multicolored masterpiece.
translator_notes: The verb ruqqam ('to embroider') is used in Exodus 26:36 and 27:16 for the embroidered screens of the tabernacle. The same artistry that decorated God's dwelling place is applied to the human body in the womb. The 'depths of the earth' is a mythological-poetic image, not a literal claim — it identifies the womb with the earth's hidden interior, the place of forming and shaping that no human eye can see.

key_terms.semantic_range: to embroider, to weave in colors, to variegate; to form with intricate design
key_terms.note: A pual (intensive passive) form — 'I was embroidered.' The verb treats the human body as a textile masterpiece, woven with the same skill and care as the tabernacle's finest curtains. It is one of the most striking metaphors for human creation in any literature.

verses.text_hebrew:
גׇּלְמִ֤י ׀ רָ֘א֤וּ עֵינֶ֗יךָ
וְעַֽל־סִפְרְךָ֮ כֻּלָּ֢ם יִכָּ֫תֵ֥בוּ
יָמִ֥ים יֻצָּ֑רוּ
ולא (וְל֖וֹ) אֶחָ֣ד בָּהֶֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: Thine eyes did see my substance, yet being unperfect; and in thy book all my members were written, which in continuance were fashioned, when as yet there was none of them.
verses.rendering:
Your eyes saw my unformed body.
In Your book they were all written —
the days that were shaped for me
when not one of them yet existed.
verses.expanded_rendering: The word golmi ('my unformed substance, my embryo') appears only here in the Hebrew Bible. It refers to the raw, unfinished body — the embryo before differentiation, the lump before it takes shape. God's eyes saw even that — the stage before the psalmist had recognizable form. Then the stunning claim: ve-al sifrrekha kullam yikkateivu ('and in Your book they were all written'). God has a book — a sefer — in which the psalmist's days (yamim) were recorded before they existed. The phrase yamim yutstsaru ('days that were formed/shaped') uses the verb yatsar ('to form, to shape' — the potter's verb from Genesis 2:7). God shaped the psalmist's days the way He shaped Adam from dust — each day is a divine creation. And the final clause: ve-lo echad bahem ('and not one of them [yet existed]'). Before a single day had occurred, all were written. God's knowledge is not retrospective but prospective — He knew the story before the first page was turned.
translator_notes: The word golem appears only here in the entire Hebrew Bible. In later Jewish tradition (especially medieval mysticism), a golem became an artificially created being — the word's biblical meaning is simply 'unformed mass, embryo, raw material.' The concept of a divine book recording future events appears in Exodus 32:32-33 (the book of life), Daniel 12:1, and Malachi 3:16.

The textual note in the Hebrew (the ketiv/qere variation between velo and velo) creates ambiguity: 'and to Him' or 'and not.' The qere reading velo ('and to Him') yields: 'and to Him [was] one of them' — meaning each day belongs to God. The ketiv reading velo ('and not') yields: 'and not one of them [yet existed].' Both readings are theologically rich.

key_terms.semantic_range: unformed mass, embryo, raw material, wrapped or folded substance
key_terms.note: A hapax legomenon — appearing only once in the Hebrew Bible. It describes the human body at its earliest, most undifferentiated stage. God saw it even then. The word later developed a rich tradition in Jewish mysticism, but its biblical meaning is simply 'the not-yet-formed.'

verses.text_hebrew:
וְלִ֗י מַה־יָּקְר֣וּ רֵעֶ֣יךָ אֵ֑ל
מֶ֥ה עָ֝צְמ֗וּ רָאשֵׁיהֶֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: How precious also are thy thoughts unto me, O God! how great is the sum of them!
verses.rendering:
How precious to me are Your thoughts, God!
How vast is the sum of them!
translator_notes: The word yaqru ('are precious, are heavy, are weighty') from yaqar describes something of great value and weight. God's re'ekha ('Your thoughts, Your purposes, Your intentions') toward the psalmist are not merely numerous but precious — each one carries weight and worth. The word rasheihem ('their sum, their heads, their chief parts') suggests the total is beyond calculation.

verses.text_hebrew:
אֶ֭סְפְּרֵם מֵח֣וֹל יִרְבּ֑וּן
הֱ֝קִיצֹ֗תִי וְעוֹדִ֥י עִמָּֽךְ׃
verses.text_kjv: If I should count them, they are more in number than the sand: when I awake, I am still with thee.
verses.rendering:
If I tried to count them, they would outnumber the sand.
When I awake, I am still with You.
verses.expanded_rendering: The impossibility of counting God's thoughts echoes the Abrahamic promise — 'count the stars, if you can' (Genesis 15:5) and 'as the sand on the seashore' (Genesis 22:17). God's thoughts toward the psalmist are as uncountable as the covenant blessings promised to Abraham. The closing phrase is one of the psalm's most beautiful: heqitsoti ve-odi immakh ('I awake, and I am still with You'). After the journey through heaven, Sheol, the wings of dawn, the far sea, darkness, and the womb, the psalmist returns to the present moment — waking up in the morning — and discovers the simplest form of omnipresence: God is here when I open my eyes. The journey through cosmic spaces ends in a bedroom at dawn.
translator_notes: The phrase ve-odi immakh ('and I am still with You') is the psalm's most intimate moment. After twenty-four lines of theological exploration, the psalmist arrives at a child's experience: I woke up and You were there. The word odi ('still, yet, I continue to be') expresses continuity — the relationship that exists in sleep persists into waking.

verses.text_hebrew:
אִם־תִּקְטֹ֥ל אֱל֗וֹהַּ רָ֫שָׁ֥ע
וְאַנְשֵׁ֥י דָ֝מִ֗ים ס֣וּרוּ מֶֽנִּי׃
verses.text_kjv: Surely thou wilt slay the wicked, O God: depart from me therefore, ye bloody men.
verses.rendering:
If only You would slay the wicked, God!
You men of bloodshed — get away from me!
translator_notes: The psalm's tone shifts abruptly. The contemplative meditation gives way to imprecation. The logic, however, follows: if God knows everything and is everywhere, why do the wicked persist? The phrase anshei damim ('men of blood/bloodshed') describes those who commit violence. The imperative suru menni ('turn aside from me, depart from me') is the psalmist's rejection of association with the violent.

verses.text_hebrew:
אֲשֶׁ֣ר יֹ֭אמְרֻךָ לִמְזִמָּ֑ה
נָשֻׂ֖א לַשָּׁ֣וְא עָרֶֽיךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: For they speak against thee wickedly, and thine enemies take thy name in vain.
verses.rendering:
They speak of You with wicked intent;
Your enemies use Your name for falsehood.
translator_notes: The wicked speak God's name la-mezimmah ('for evil purpose, with wicked intent') and nasa la-shav ('lift up for emptiness/falsehood'). The charge is blasphemy — using God's name to serve lies. The language echoes the third commandment: 'You shall not lift up (tissa) the name of the LORD your God for emptiness (la-shav)' (Exodus 20:7).

verses.text_hebrew:
הֲלוֹא־מְשַׂנְאֶ֖יךָ יְהוָ֥ה ׀ אֶשְׂנָ֑א
וּ֝בִתְקוֹמְמֶ֗יךָ אֶתְקוֹטָֽט׃
verses.text_kjv: Do not I hate them, O LORD, that hate thee? and am not I grieved with those that rise up against thee?
verses.rendering:
Do I not hate those who hate You, LORD?
Do I not loathe those who rise against You?
translator_notes: The psalmist claims solidarity with God against God's enemies. The verb esna ('I hate') applied to God's enemies is the mirror of mesanekha ('those who hate You'). The verb etqotat ('I loathe, I am disgusted, I contend with') is from a root meaning 'to feel nausea, to be revolted.' The rhetoric is: my enemies are Your enemies; my hatred aligns with Your justice.

verses.text_hebrew:
תַּכְלִ֣ית שִׂנְאָ֣ה שְׂנֵאתִ֑ים
לְ֝אוֹיְבִ֗ים הָ֣יוּ לִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: I hate them with perfect hatred: I count them mine enemies.
verses.rendering:
With complete hatred I hate them;
they have become my own enemies.
translator_notes: The phrase takhlit sinah ('complete/perfect hatred, hatred to the utmost') is the strongest possible expression of animosity in Hebrew. Takhlit means 'end, completion, perfection' — this hatred holds nothing back. The psalmist claims his enemies are God's enemies, and therefore his hatred is righteous alignment rather than personal malice. The following verses will test this claim.

verses.text_hebrew:
חׇקְרֵ֣נִי אֵ֭ל וְדַ֣ע לְבָבִ֑י
בְּ֝חָנֵ֗נִי וְדַ֣ע שַׂרְעַפָּֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Search me, O God, and know my heart: try me, and know my thoughts:
verses.rendering:
Search me, God, and know my heart;
test me and know my anxious thoughts.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm returns to its opening theme, but now as invitation rather than declaration. Verse 1 stated 'You have searched me and known me' (past tense, accomplished fact); verse 23 requests 'Search me and know my heart' (imperative, invited act). The psalmist, having claimed to hate God's enemies with perfect hatred, now submits himself to the very omniscience he has celebrated — examine me, see if my hatred is truly righteous or secretly self-serving. The verb chaqreni ('search me') is the same chaqar from verse 1. The word sar'appai ('my anxious thoughts, my disquieting thoughts') is rare and refers to the troubled, branching thoughts that keep a person awake — the mental restlessness that may conceal hidden motives. The psalmist does not trust his own self-assessment; he invites the all-knowing God to do what only God can do: see the truth beneath the surface.
translator_notes: The word sar'appai (from sar'af or sa'if, 'disquieting thought, anxious rumination') appears only here and in Psalm 94:19. It describes the branching, intrusive thoughts that trouble the inner life — what modern psychology might call anxious rumination. The psalmist asks God to search even these turbulent, half-formed thoughts.

verses.text_hebrew:
וּרְאֵ֗ה אִם־דֶּ֣רֶךְ עֹ֣צֶב בִּ֑י
וּ֝נְחֵ֗נִי בְּדֶ֣רֶךְ עוֹלָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: And see if there be any wicked way in me, and lead me in the way everlasting.
verses.rendering:
See if there is any hurtful way in me,
and lead me in the way that lasts forever.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm's final verse is a prayer of radical vulnerability. The phrase derekh otsev ('way of pain, hurtful way, grievous path') may mean 'a way that causes pain to others,' 'a way rooted in idolatry' (from etsev, 'idol'), or 'a way that leads to grief.' The psalmist asks God to identify any such path within him — any hidden pattern of living that causes harm. The alternative is derekh olam ('the way everlasting, the ancient way, the way that endures'). The word olam can mean 'eternal' or 'ancient' — both work: the psalmist asks to be led in the way that has always been true and will always be true, the path that does not expire. The psalm that began with 'You have searched me' ends with 'lead me' — from the fact of being known to the prayer for being guided. Knowledge without guidance would be surveillance; guidance without knowledge would be superficial. The psalmist wants both: full exposure and faithful direction.
translator_notes: The phrase derekh otsev is ambiguous. If from etsev ('idol'), it means 'a way of idolatry' — any hidden idol-worship in the psalmist's life. If from etsev ('pain, grief'), it means 'a way that causes pain' or 'a way that leads to sorrow.' The ambiguity may be productive — the psalmist asks God to find whatever is wrong, however it should be categorized.

The phrase derekh olam ('way everlasting') echoes Jeremiah 6:16: 'Stand at the crossroads and look; ask for the ancient paths (netivot olam), where the good way is, and walk in it.' The everlasting way is not a new discovery but a return to what has always been true.
